title: mfa — authorization policies
description: Two policies. MfaPolicy governs the self-service surface — viewing / enrolling / disabling one's own MFA methods. Every write is additionally gated by `mfa.require_recent_challenge` middleware so a stolen session cookie can't disable MFA without proving fresh possession of the primary factor. WebauthnCredentialPolicy governs the per-credential CRUD on registered hardware keys — same principle: caller must own the Identity that owns the credential. Platform-admin abilities (force-disable, regenerate-codes for a user) are on MfaPolicy too, gated behind the `platform_admin` guard.

policies.model: Mfa (virtual — represents the caller's own MFA posture, not a persisted row)

abilities.scope: Caller reads their own MFA posture (methods enrolled, recovery codes remaining). Always allowed for the resolved caller — the permission exists so a UI can render / hide the MFA panel.
Caller enrolls a TOTP secret on their own Identity. Additionally gated by the `mfa_totp` entitlement + `mfa.enrollment_enabled` feature flag.
abilities.reason: First-time enrollment cannot require a challenge — that would be a chicken-and-egg problem.

abilities.scope: Caller disables the TOTP factor on their own Identity.
Caller registers a new WebAuthn credential. Additionally gated by the `mfa_webauthn` entitlement.
abilities.condition_note: Requires a recent challenge ONLY if the Identity already has one MFA factor enrolled. First-time WebAuthn registration on an Identity with no other MFA has no prior factor to challenge against — treat as first-time enrollment.

abilities.scope: Caller renames / disables / deletes their own WebAuthn credentials. Enforced via WebauthnCredentialPolicy below (relationship-scoped).
Caller regenerates their recovery-code set — old set purged, new set emitted once.
Caller re-downloads their current recovery-code set. Audit-logged as a data-egress event.
Platform support views a user's MFA posture. Redacted response — no credential_id_b64url, no plaintext, no bytea public keys.

Platform-security force-disables MFA for a user during account-recovery flows. Cascades: TOTP secret nulled, every WebAuthn credential disabled + retention-purged after 90 days. Fires MfaDisabled(trigger=admin_action). Requires justification; audit-logged as critical.
Platform support regenerates recovery codes for a user + emails them the new codes. Used when a user has lost their MFA device AND their recovery-code storage. Audit-logged as warn.
Caller lists their own registered credentials (`identity_id = current_identity.id`).
Caller reads ONE of their own credentials (relationship-checked in the policy before the field-level authorization).
Caller renames or disables their own credential. Delete is a separate ability (below).
Caller hard-deletes their own credential BEFORE the 90-day retention window. Fires WebauthnCredentialDeleted(trigger=user_action).
Platform support lists a user's registered credentials for audit / debugging.
Platform-security disables a credential during an incident (e.g. reported stolen laptop). Cascade fires WebauthnCredentialDisabled(reason=admin_force_disable). Audit-critical.

boundary_note: This policy family spans BOTH guards — MfaPolicy carries `viewOwn`/`enrollTotp`/`disableTotp`/etc. on the `sanctum` guard AND `platformForceDisable`/`platformRegenerateRecoveryCodes` on the `platform_admin` guard. Guard-namespace isolation lives at the permission-string level (`mfa.*` vs `platform.mfa.*`) — a `sanctum` role never grants a `platform.mfa.*` permission (see identity/access's `GuardMismatch` (422) at write time). The dual-guard nature is unavoidable because MFA is the ONE substrate every principal class touches; tenant users manage their own MFA, and platform staff manage recovery paths across every tenant.
recent_challenge_gate: The `mfa.require_recent_challenge` middleware (see middleware.json) reads the caller's session for a `last_mfa_challenge_at` timestamp. Actions marked `requires_recent_challenge: true` refuse when `now() - last_mfa_challenge_at > 5 minutes` with error `MFA_CHALLENGE_TOO_STALE` (403). The caller is redirected to a step-up challenge flow, verifies fresh, retries the original request. This closes the 'stolen session' attack — even with a valid Sanctum PAT, disabling MFA or regenerating codes requires proving current possession of the primary factor.
first_time_enrollment_note: Actions marked `requires_recent_challenge: 'conditional'` handle the special first-time-enrollment case: an Identity with zero MFA factors cannot produce a challenge (there's nothing to challenge against). The middleware evaluates `Identity.mfa_enabled` on the caller and only enforces recent-challenge when true. First-time enrollment is subject only to the `identity/auth`-owned session-freshness rule (default 15 minutes since last password prompt) — see identity/auth's step-up-authentication design.
